transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: It is early spring in Bellwether: chilly mornings, showers, bright intervals and occasional late frost.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: The bus leaves you at Bellwether just after nine. Its engine noise thins along the road until the village is quiet again.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: For now, there is the road ahead, your suitcase at your feet, and Eleanor's key in your pocket.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You wait beneath the shelter. Traffic is sparse enough that every engine can be heard before it appears.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You stop for a while and listen. Bellwether is quiet, but never entirely still.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You wait beneath the shelter. Traffic is sparse enough that every engine can be heard before it appears.

transcript.speaker: Observation
transcript.text: The road beyond the bus stop has begun to feel less like the way out and more like one edge of Bellwether.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You slow down and pay attention to the place rather than merely passing through it.

transcript.speaker: Notebook
transcript.text: The road has a rhythm: Long quiet gaps separate the traffic; regular vehicles are easy to distinguish from unfamiliar ones.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You make your way to Village Road.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: The road has settled into a quieter interval.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You pick up a few ordinary household necessities. It is a small thing, but carrying them back makes your stay feel less temporary.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: Mrs Ellis is asking a neighbour after someone she knows.

transcript.text: You linger without a destination and watch Bellwether carry on around you.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You make your way to Bellwether Bakery.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: The bakery counter is staffed and warm with work.

transcript.speaker: Jonah
transcript.text: You must be Eleanor's relation. I had a loaf riding on whether you'd take the morning bus.

transcript.speaker: You
transcript.text: People talk about strangers here, then?

transcript.speaker: Jonah
transcript.text: Only until they stop being strangers. Around here, that rarely takes long.

transcript.speaker: Jonah
transcript.text: Here. Bread. Consider it an apology from the village gossip network.

transcript.speaker: Journal
transcript.text: Side Story completed: A Warm Welcome.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You spend £2 on something warm and eat without rushing.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: Jonah is taking a few minutes of fresh air.

transcript.text: You slow down and pay attention to the place rather than merely passing through it.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: Stone cottages lean toward the narrow road. Shop windows, garden gates and the turn toward the church give the village its daily rhythm.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: The village has grown busier around the green and shops. A cyclist rattles past with a basket on the rear rack. Blackbirds work through the wet grass.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: The road has settled into a quieter interval.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: Delivery van is nearby, resuming the morning delivery round.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You make your way to Bellwether Bakery.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: The bakery counter is staffed and warm with work.

transcript.speaker: You
transcript.text: Morning. How are you?

transcript.speaker: Jonah
transcript.text: Hello. Good to see you.

transcript.speaker: You
transcript.text: How has your day been?

transcript.speaker: Jonah
transcript.text: Sorry, I lost the thread for a moment. Go on.

transcript.label: Sit by the Window and Watch the Street

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: You take a place by the window. Bellwether passes in fragments: shopping bags, bicycle wheels, a dog that knows where it is going.

transcript.speaker: Bellwether
transcript.text: Jonah is checking the ovens and turning the next batch.

transcript.speaker: Observation
transcript.text: You are learning the bakery's quieter intervals as well as its busy ones.

transcript.text: You review the last few things you thought worth remembering.
